text:
Here's one: "A clock that strikes thirteen is not broken until you have counted."

The proverb works against our instinct to dismiss what first appears absurd. A clock striking thirteen seems like obvious malfunction, the very emblem of something gone wrong, and our reflex is to write it off the moment we hear that impossible thirteenth chime. But the saying insists on a discipline of attention: you have not actually established that the clock is broken until you have done the counting yourself, deliberately, all the way through. The thirteenth strike might be your own miscount, an echo, two clocks overlapping, or a sign that the hour itself is stranger than you assumed.

There is also a sharper, more unsettling reading buried in it, which is the one I prefer. A clock that strikes thirteen casts doubt backward over the twelve strikes you already trusted. If the instrument can be wrong now, it could have been wrong before, and the only way to know is to have been counting all along rather than nodding along to chimes you took for granted. In that sense the proverb is about the cost of inattention: the people who are startled by the thirteenth strike are precisely those who never bothered to count the first twelve.

So you might offer it to someone who leaps to judgment at the first odd signal, or equally to someone who has placed too much faith in a source simply because it has seemed reliable up to now. It rewards patience and suspicion in equal measure, which is the mark of a good proverb. It tells you neither to trust nor to dismiss, but to count.
